title: The normal condition for sample proportions
description: Practice determining if the sampling distribution of a sample proportion is skewed or approximately normal.
topic_path: Sampling distribution of a sample proportion
The normal condition for sample proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
Emiliana runs a restaurant that receives a shipment of $50$ tangerines every day. According to the supplier, approximately $12\%$ of the population of these tangerines is overripe. Suppose that Emiliana calculates the daily proportion of overripe tangerines in her sample of $50$. We can assume the supplier's claim is true, and that the tangerines each day represent a random sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the daily proportions of overripe tangerines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=50$ tangerines, and $p=0.12$ since $12\%$ of the population is overripe.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=50(0.12)=6<10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=50(1-0.12)=44\geq10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $6$ overripe tangerines in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting an overripe tangerine is relatively low, we expect a small percentage of the daily sample to be overripe on most days. Some days however could have a relatively larger percentage of overripe tangerines.

So the proportion of overripe tangerines will be low most days, and skewed toward the higher proportions.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of the daily proportions of overripe tangerines will be skewed to the right.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a Gallup poll in 2006, about $10\%$ of Americans said they were "very afraid" of flying on an airplane. Suppose that we took random samples of $n=40$ people from this population and computed the proportion of people in each sample who were very afraid of flying. 

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of the proportion of people who were very afraid of flying?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=40$ people, and $p=0.10$ since $10\%$ of the population is very afraid of flying.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=40(0.10)=4<10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=40(1-0.10)=36\geq10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $4$ people who are very afraid of flying in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a very afraid person is relatively low, we expect a small percentage of the sample to be very afraid most of the time. Some samples however could have a relatively larger percentage of very afraid people.

So the proportion of people who are very afraid will be low in most samples, and skewed toward the higher proportions.

###Answer

![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f656b9f92988450c3d11a26384679d0a8197e1b)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2189ce50b630ad5301da6ba74453363f4ff55817)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f656b9f92988450c3d11a26384679d0a8197e1b)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/42feba334805901a5535027d829126b292d1b5d9)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2a8d21107373ec7851248badfbbcfb5a2f7c416f)

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a Nielsen survey, $92\%$ of consumers around the world say that they trust recommendations from friends and family most, above all other forms of advertising. Suppose we took random samples of $n=100$ consumers from this population and computed the proportion of consumers in each sample who trust recommendations from friends and family most. 

[//]: (http://www.nielsen.com/us/en/insights/reports/2012/global-trust-in-advertising-and-brand-messages.html)

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the proportions of consumers who trust recommendations from friends and family most?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=100$ consumers, and $p=0.92$ since $92\%$ of the population trusts recommendations from family and friends most.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=100(0.92)=92\geq 10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=100(1-0.92)=8<10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $8$ consumers who do not trust recommendations from friends and family the most in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a consumer who trusts recommendations from family and friends the most is relatively high, we expect a large percentage of most of the samples to trust that recommendation most. Some samples however could have a relatively lower percentage of consumers who trust recommendations from family and friends the most.

So the proportion of consumers who trust recommendations from family and friends the most will be high in most samples and skewed toward the lower proportions.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of the proportions of consumers who trust recommendations from family and friends the most will be skewed to the left.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain city's Department of Transportation (DOT) repairs about $80{,}000$ potholes per year. The DOT completes $90\%$ of the repairs on the reported potholes within two weeks of the report. Suppose the DOT annually takes a random sample of $75$ of the reported potholes and calculates the proportion $\hat p$ of potholes repaired within two weeks.

[//]: (Percent from https://comptroller.nyc.gov/reports/audit-report-on-the-department-of-transportations-tracking-of-pothole-repairs/ , but used a much smaller number of potholes per year.)

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=75$ potholes, and $p=0.9$ since $90\%$ of the population is repaired within two weeks of the report.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=75(0.9)=67.5\geq 10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=75(1-0.9)=7.5<10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $7.5$ potholes that are not repaired within two weeks in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a reported pothole that is repaired within two weeks of the report is relatively high, we expect a large percentage of most of the samples to be repaired within two weeks. Some samples however could have a relatively lower percentage of potholes repaired within two weeks of the report.

So the proportion of reported potholes that are repaired within two weeks of the report will be high in most samples and skewed toward the lower proportions.

###Answer

![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7e0cc2c901d59ff3cb66f95c65b53945941190ce)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7e0cc2c901d59ff3cb66f95c65b53945941190ce)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cd3c04029639e8188f562d43e669418894831902)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afc8ae1993693bea1c63e41032ceef50f5c14e35)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/28ab8b416dafe051b6acdf74c1764d94c94ba328)

assessment_items.question_content:
According to the admissions director of a certain college, approximately $11\%$ of the $250$ freshmen admitted had applied using the early decision option. Suppose that we took random samples of $20$ freshmen from this population and computed the proportion $\hat p$ of freshmen in each sample who had applied using the early decision option. We can assume the admissions director's claim is true.

[//]: (http://inlikeme.com/admission-statistics-acceptance-rates-early-vs-regular/)

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=20$ freshmen, and $p=0.11$ since $11\%$ of the population applied using the early decision option.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=20(0.11)=2.2<10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=20(1-0.11)=17.8\geq10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect about $2$ freshmen who applied using the early decision option in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a freshman who used the early decision option is relatively low, we expect a small percentage of the sample to have used the early decision option in each sample. Some samples, however, could have a relatively larger percentage of freshmen who used the early decision option.

So the proportion $\hat p$ will be low in most samples and skewed toward the higher proportions.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat p$ will be skewed to the right.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a newspaper survey, about $9\%$ of the local residents order groceries online at least once a month. Suppose we took random samples of $n=80$ residents from this population and computed the proportion $\hat p$ of residents in each sample who order groceries online at least once a month. We can assume the newspaper's claim is true.

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=80$ residents, and $p=0.09$ since $9\%$ of the population orders groceries online at least once a month.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=80(0.09)=7.2<10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=80(1-0.09)=72.8\geq10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect about $7$ residents who order groceries online at least once a month in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a resident who orders groceries online is relatively low, most of the time we expect a small percentage of the sample to order groceries online. Some samples however could have a relatively larger percentage of residents who order groceries online at least once a month.

So the proportion of $\hat p$ will be low in most samples, and skewed toward the higher proportions.

###Answer

![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.09.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aadacea38ef41b572ea1c22d1a9b0261511ccd4c)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.09.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/784f50cd76a4320844366e63291069effbfaeb6d)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.09.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aadacea38ef41b572ea1c22d1a9b0261511ccd4c)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.09.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cfabf487a0d6162bdc6c72da1ae4979671c12de)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.09.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f878102d9336638aefcff27e6f2058981417892)

assessment_items.question_content:
A map website claims to be available $99.995\%$ of the time (measured in minute long increments). Suppose that we took random samples of $n=1000$ minutes from the population of minutes in a year and computed the proportion of minutes in each sample for which the website was available. We can assume the website's claim is accurate.

[//]: (http://www.zdnet.com/article/google-maps-now-covers-75-of-global-pop-26-million-miles-of-directions/)

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the proportions of minutes in which the website is available?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=1000$ minutes, and $p=0.99995$ since $99.995\%$ of the population of minutes the website is available.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=1000(0.99995)=999.95\geq 10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=1000(1-0.99995)=0.05<10
\end{align}$

Since we expect fewer than $1$ minute in which the website is unavailable in the sample, which is less than $10$, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a minute in which the website is available is relatively high, we expect a large percentage of most of the samples to have the website available. Some samples however could have a relatively lower percentage of minutes in which the website is available.

So the proportion of minutes in which the website is available will be high in most samples and skewed toward the lower proportions.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of the proportions of minutes in which the website is available will be skewed to the left.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control inspector routinely takes random samples of $200$ cans of fruit cocktail produced in a packing plant and calculates the proportion $\hat p$ of cans from each sample with at least $3$ cherries. Suppose that $98\%$ of fruit cocktail cans produced in that packing plant contain at least $3$ cherries. 

[//]: (https://www.ams.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media/Canned_Fruit_Cocktail_Inspection_Instructions%5B1%5D.pdf, but sample size would usually be 100 cans.)

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=200$ cans, and $p=0.98$ since $98\%$ of the population contains at least $3$ cherries.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=200(0.98)=196\geq 10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=200(1-0.98)=4<10
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $4$ cans with fewer than $3$ cherries in the sample, which is less than $10$ cans, the sample proportions will not be normally distributed.

###What direction is the skew?

Since the probability of getting a can with at least $3$ cherries is relatively high, we expect a large percentage of most of the samples to have at least $3$ cherries. Some samples however could have a relatively lower percentage of cans with at least $3$ cherries.

So the proportion of $\hat p$ will be high in most samples and skewed toward the lower proportions.

###Answer

![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.98.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8cf8139f8736ef2e7cdddb59ca86207f6ee7cc9)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.98.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8cf8139f8736ef2e7cdddb59ca86207f6ee7cc9)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.98.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/72036fa0f90f24de7b8a40b3014b05212892cb49)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.98.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/618aae3ad9eb0b136cad366645ccafba65449645)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.98.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5d97c2b0ec264325420591fb2ea7d7803ccef310)

assessment_items.question_content:
Toni runs a restaurant that receives a shipment of $200$ oranges every day. According to the supplier, approximately $11\%$ of the population of these oranges is overripe. Suppose that Toni calculates the daily proportion of overripe oranges in her sample of $200$. We can assume the supplier's claim is true, and that the oranges each day represent a random sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the daily proportions of overripe oranges?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=200$ oranges, and $p=0.11$ since $11\%$ of the population is overripe.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=200(0.11)=22\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=200(1-0.11)=178\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected overripe and at least $10$ not overripe per sample.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of the daily proportions of overripe oranges will be approximately normal.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
A college has a limited enrollment summer course that only accepts $50$ students, selected at random from the more than $2000$ qualified students who apply each year. Of these applicants, $22\%$ have completed $2$ or fewer years of college. Suppose the registrar calculates the annual proportion $\hat p$ of students accepted into the course who have completed $2$ or fewer years of college.

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=50$ students, and $p=0.22$ since $22\%$ of the population has completed $2$ or fewer years of college.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=50(0.22)=11\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=50(1-0.22)=39\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected with $2$ or fewer years of college completed and at least $10$ expected with more than $2$ years of college completed per sample.

###Answer

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.22.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2a483a4d90928332b943dd201530090b70c5ccb2)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.22.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0ac64bb1f7bd87c03a8fa8ec36b40908f74ad4eb)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.22.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29f68abaa257d47cc631fa019afa03dcb6a66acf)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.22.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2a483a4d90928332b943dd201530090b70c5ccb2)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.22.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f72417c5772ef350581fdd4c4b2c3238a2380a49)

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a Nielsen survey, radio reaches $88\%$ of children each week. Suppose we took weekly random samples of $n=125$ children from this population and computed the proportion of children in each sample whom radio reaches. 

[//]: (http://www.nielsen.com/us/en/insights/news/2017/sounds-like-teen-spirit.html)

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the proportions of children the radio reaches?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=125$ children, and $p=0.88$ since radio reaches $88\%$ of the population.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=125(0.88)=110\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=125(1-0.88)=15\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected reached and at least $10$ expected not reached per sample.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of the weekly proportions of children the radio reaches will be approximately normal.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
According to the town gardening association, $86\%$ of the over $15{,}000$ families with vegetable gardens in the town grow tomatoes. Suppose we took random samples of $100$ families each from this population and calculated the proportion $\hat p$ of sampled families who grow tomatoes for each sample.

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=100$ families, and $p=0.86$ since $86\%$ of the population grows tomatoes.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=100(0.86)=86\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=100(1-0.86)=14\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected who grow tomatoes and at least $10$ expected who don't grow tomatoes per sample.

###Answer

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.86.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3225ef3859d5d8984220d4801870cb0f842a4886)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.86.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e055d5a8f2b3fcade712d34219a04d1b2c376258)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.86.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/20266acb6ac8e3521d4a01dd6a8a1d6e267ecb88)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.86.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3225ef3859d5d8984220d4801870cb0f842a4886)

assessment_items.question_content:
In the year 2012, $12\%$ of the residents of the US moved to a different residence. Suppose that several census workers took random samples of $n=150$ residents from the population and computed the proportion $\hat p$ of residents in each sample who had moved in 2012.

[//]: (https://www.census.gov/library/publications/2014/demo/p20-574.html)

**What would be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=150$ residents, and $p=0.12$ since $12\%$ of the population moved to a different residence.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=150(0.12)=18\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=150(1-0.12)=132\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected residents who moved and at least $10$ expected residents who didn't move per sample.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat p$ will be approximately normal.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
Deshad runs a river tubing company that serves $300$ customers a day during sunny, summer days. He knows that $45\%$ of the customers rent life jackets. Suppose that Deshad calculates the daily proportion $\hat p$ of customers who rent life jackets from his sample of $300$. We can assume the customers each day represent a random sample.

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=300$ customers, and $p=0.45$ since $45\%$ of the population rents life jackets.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=300(0.45)=135\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=300(1-0.45)=165\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected customers who rent life jackets and at least $10$ expected customers who don't rent life jackets per sample.

###Answer

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/981f1e3f65748739faec7e60472102b3a3e7752b)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0184076b6fe7d8de5eef93ea5b68dce4101d20f0)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/254818530a88cb91e8dfd02e78ec0e08741bc44a)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/981f1e3f65748739faec7e60472102b3a3e7752b)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.45.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b640d2c7d3f579166f2398be84c0fec010935309)

assessment_items.question_content:
In one community, $78\%$ of the over $400{,}000$ people who speak Spanish are native speakers. Suppose a politician took monthly random samples of $75$ people in the community who speak Spanish and calculated the proportion $\hat p$ of people from each sample who were native Spanish speakers.

[//]: (http://nypost.com/2015/06/29/us-has-more-spanish-speakers-than-spain/ percents for US)

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=75$ people, and $p=0.78$ since $78\%$ of the Spanish speakers in the community are native speakers.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=75(0.78)=58.5\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=75(1-0.78)=16.5\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected native speakers and at least $10$ expected nonnative speakers per sample.

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat p$ will be approximately normal.

choices.content: Skewed to the left
Skewed to the right
Approximately normal
Uniform

assessment_items.question_content:
A band selects $40$ ticket-holders at random from each concert to win t-shirts. The band knows that $55\%$ of the ticket-holders buy their tickets at least a month before the concert. Suppose the band calculated the proportion $\hat p$ of winners from each concert who bought their tickets at least a month before the concert.

**Which of the following distributions is the best approximation of the sampling distribution of $\hat p$?**  
*Each distribution uses the same scale.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Is the sampling distribution approximately normal?

We need at least $10$ expected "successes" and at least $10$ expected "failures"  for the sampling distribution of the sample proportion to be approximately normal:

$np\geq10\quad\text{and}\quad n\left(1-p\right)\geq10$ 

Our sample size is $n=40$ ticket-holders, and $p=0.55$ since $55\%$ of the ticket-holders buy their tickets at least a month before the concert.

$\begin{align}
&\text{Expected successes: }np=40(0.55)=22\geq10 \\\\
&\text{Expected failures: }n(1-p)=40(1-0.55)=18\geq10
\end{align}$

We have at least $10$ expected who bought their tickets at least a month before the concert and at least $10$ expected who bought their tickets within the month.

###Answer

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.55.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62ac0adcb71b63d622db8c6aaef47e199fb46166)

choices.content: ![A left skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.55.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f0216609a7d2757f1f4ef2cdd2436dd7a3945275)
![A right skewed distribution curve has a mean of 0.55.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/709406f66128bfea0155efdd18c1c86e060c3464)
![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 0.55.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/62ac0adcb71b63d622db8c6aaef47e199fb46166)
![A horizontal, uniform distribution has a mean of 0.55.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cffc7b31bedd155b54c5666eb15d739a3764198b)
